segments.text: Hi, I'm Tariq, CEO of Sonar. I'm so
thankful that you've chosen to join us
at Sonar Summit. We have an amazing
lineup for you. Speakers from Sonar,
thought leaders from around the world,
and representatives from our customers
and partners.
We hope that you'll find it helpful,
that you'll learn something new and walk
away with a few ideas.
Many of you are longtime Sonar users. I
want to say at the outset on behalf of
all Sonar sourcers, thank you. Sonar has
been a developer oriented company since
our beginning as an open source project.
And today there's over 7 million of you
around the world who use Sonar to
improve trust and software development.
We wouldn't be here without you.
For those who are new to Sonar, who
haven't spoken to us in a while, let me
tell you a little about us. As I
mentioned, we started life as an open
source project. Our core platform, SonarQube,
has over 7 million developers
using us on-prem, in the cloud, and in
their IDE.
We helped to pioneer the code
verification space. And thanks to all of
you, we're the industry's leading trust
and verification platform for code.
We work with you across over 40
different programming languages from
Python to COBOL and employ a range of
sophisticated mathematical reasoning
algorithms to help detect over 7,000
different types of issues that could
exist in your code. These algorithms
help make your code better, more
reliable, more readable, more
maintainable, lower tech debt, less
complex, and more secure. The analysis
is fast, it is deterministic, so you get
the same result every time. It is
transparent so you know exactly what was
checked, what was found and why. It is
comprehensive looking at your entire
codebase and understanding not just the
literal code but the architecture and the
data flows. Really whatever helps you
verify and trust your code.
We help you define quality profiles.
What are the standards you, your
regulators, your auditors, your
executives need to meet? This could be
because of best practice, personal
preference, or compliance. Whether
you're looking to certify for MISRA,
NIST, secure software development
framework, SOC 2, or a range of other
regulations,
we help you enforce these profiles with
quality gates, ensuring that the code
that you or your AI checks in meets the
standards you've set. We do this with
the lowest false positive rates in the
industry, less than 3%. And we do it
with advice, guidance, and AI-powered
suggestions about how you can address
any issues that are found. You'll hear a
lot more about our capabilities and
roadmap over the course of the summit.
We are very fortunate and again thankful
that hundreds of thousands of instances
exist inside of organizations; that over
25,000 companies choose to use our
enterprise capabilities including 75% of
the Fortune 100, 19 of the top 20 banks
in the world outside of China, leading
technology companies like Nvidia, top
retailers and healthcare providers,
public sector institutions from around
the world. On behalf of our customers,
Sonar analyzes over 750 billion lines of
code every day. Trillions of lines of
code every quarter. It doesn't matter
whether you wrote the code yourself or
whether ChatGPT, Cursor, Claude,
Codeex, Devin, Copilot wrote the code.
Leveraging our MCP server, APIs, and
soon our CLI tools, we are integrated
with virtually all major AI coding
systems and with systems of record you
rely on: JFrog, ServiceNow, Jira,
GitHub, GitLab, you name it. And the
goal of all of that is simple. Reliable,
maintainable, understandable, secure
code built right at the start. One stat
brings this to life: Sonar customers
report that they are 44% less likely to
experience an outage due to AI compared
to people who do not use Sonar. 44%
less. Verified code is safer, more
secure, more reliable. AI models are
great, but the old expression has never
been more true: Trust but verify. Or as
we sometimes like to say: vibe then
And now, as proud as we are of
everything I just said, we know we can't
rest on our laurels. We've been doing a
lot of thinking about the future of
software development. And I want to
spend the rest of my time sharing my
thoughts with you. It's important,
really critical to say that nobody knows
where this is heading, but I think we
can make some educated guesses. So let's
get started and I'm going to start with
a provocative statement: The era of
continuous integration with its familiar
processes and workflows is rapidly
coming to an end. Traditional CI relies
on developers making small frequent
iterative commits. That continuous part
is changing. Agents don't work like
that. They operate in asynchronous
batches, often working for long periods
before dropping massive complex payloads
of code. We are seeing the emergence of
a new paradigm that will fundamentally
reshape how we create software. We're
calling this agent centric development
for good reason. There's a lot of
discussion and adoption of code
generation tools and agents. These have
undeniable strengths that are
transforming how developers do their
job. But there's a growing consensus
that developers will be focusing more on
design, architecture, and planning and
then on monitoring, verification, and
review.
Less discussed are the changes required
to ensure that the software development
agents themselves are operating in a
trustworthy, consistent, transparent,
and responsible manner. Even in the best
hands, AI slop is pervasive. Our
research has demonstrated that left
unchecked, coding models generate
verbose, complex, buggy, and insecure
code. Agentic development requires a
strong, deliberate, intentional set of
practices and well-constructed set of
tools. These provide guardrails,
transparency, assurances and verification
necessary to build world-class software.
We call this the agent centric
development life cycle or AC/DC.
Yes, it is electrifying. That is my
first and hopefully last dad joke this
talk.
This model operates on a different set
of steps than the legacy CI model because
the continuous human cadence is gone.
Agents work for a longer period of
time before they're actually ready to
commit code. Code generation is fast and
the code is verbose. Pull requests are
much larger and more complex. Small
errors that the agent makes early in
their process compound, making the
process inherently unstable. It is much
harder to understand the changes that
are being made to the codebase,
presenting long-term risks and
challenges. Security issues, for
example, could creep in without being
noticed when 40,000 lines are being
checked in versus 200 or 300. But even
with agents writing the code, developers
are still responsible, not just for the
code—they're responsible for shipping
something that works. Activities that
used to happen post the build stage of
CI/CD, such as dynamic testing, will now
happen in the sandbox, will happen as
the code is being written and be the
developer's responsibility. The speed
and volume is overwhelming. Traditional
manual code review approaches just don't
work. This is not the normal shift left.
It is more akin to being in the matrix.
There is no right in the traditional
pipeline. Before any code is checked in,
there has to be the proof of a verified
high-quality product. Because the
continuous micro commit is dead,
production grade validation must happen
before the code is submitted. In an
agentic world, the paradigm
fundamentally needs to change. We define
the agent centric development cycle,
AC/DC, as having four discrete stages.
These stages are: guide, generate,
verify, and solve. AC/DC happens both in
the inner loop and the outer loop. That
is: guide, generate, verify, and solve
happens in each agentic reasoning loop,
ensuring that the agent stays on track
as it methodically works to achieve the
plans. These are essentially micro-
adjustments that are continuously made
using guardrails, prompt traces, rapid
verification analysis;
and then guide, generate, verify and
solve happens once the agent has
finished its work. Here the more
comprehensive verification occurs and
often the agent will have to fix larger-
scale issues that are identified. So
let's take a step back and examine the
critical parts of the AC/DC. Most
companies we see have multiple coding
Copilot, Claude Code, Lovable, Devin, and
Codeex all frequently coexist, used for
different use cases just based on
developer preference. Everyone wants to
trust these agents, but there are
examples everywhere that show that you
just can't. Blind trust is not an
option. Every company will need a
platform that feeds the generation
tools, that guides them, that verifies
them, and solves the complex problems
that arise. So, let's start with Guide.
Guiding is not just about pointing to a
codebase. It's about defining the
playing field and setting the rules of
engagement. Agents need to be told the
context and the constraints that will
shape their work. This is critical in
both greenfield and brownfield
environments to ensure that
Agents need to know, of course, what the
specifications are, but that's just the
start. They need to understand the
standards, the regulations, the
guidelines, and the guardrails you've
established for your codebase along with
the current and desired architecture.
After Guide, then you have to verify. The
code is generated, but AI makes mistakes—
lots of them. Unlike developers,
they don't make basic mistakes very often;
instead they make very complex, hard-to-
find mistakes. And the models themselves
are both unpredictable due to their
probabilistic nature and very sensitive
to changes in their training set and
environments. A prompt that worked well
yesterday has no guarantee of working
well today. Anthropic themselves
published a terrific paper about four
months ago showing how sensitive models
are to data poisoning. Given these stark
realities, verification must be
thorough, transparent, and consistent.
It has to happen in the inner loop as
the agents look to continuously
self-verify during the reasoning process
itself. It then has to happen in the
outer loop. Once the agent believes it
has constructed a good solution, the
developer must have the confidence that
the work achieves the intended
functional and non-functional outcomes:
that it meets the internal standards,
compliance requirements, that it's
maintainable, that it does the job it
was intended to do with consistency,
with evidence, and with enterprise audit
trails. Not just code review, but
rigorous, transparent, deterministic,
comprehensive verification. But no
matter how much we verify, problems are
inevitable. We have to expect that some
things will slip through. The Solve
phase is the automatic debugging and
remediation based on verification
feedback. Armed with a deep
understanding of the application
structure and the results from the
verification phase, corrections can be
made. And unlike most traditional
processes, failure is not just a bug to
be patched. It's a lesson that refines
the next iteration, making the entire
system more resilient. Many of the
traditional SDLC solutions will need to
evolve quickly or be increasingly
irrelevant as agents take over the
development process.
Critical components of the new AC/DC
cycle include first: you need an agentic
development environment. That is an
environment in which the Guide-Generate-
Verify-Solve loops can work for all your
agents regardless of what agent and code
generation partner you use. Then you
need to have a context engine. You need
to have the tools that can provide
useful context. For example, a thorough
evaluation of your codebase architecture
and crisp, transparent, and specific
articulations of the guardrails and
standards you're looking to achieve. You
need to determine which pieces of
context should be provided in each
circumstance. Too much context, too
little context, or incorrect context can
all degrade the performance instead of
enhancing it. And then last, but
certainly not least, and perhaps most
important, you need a trust and
verification platform. Software
development worked because, generally
speaking, companies trusted that
developers were going to write good code
and review that code. Verification was
important, but many treated it as
optional given trust was so high.
Agent-centric development breaks that
compact. AI assisted and agentic
workflows create code at such high
volumes and speed that pull requests are
10x or larger than in the past. Truly
understanding the new code is almost
impossible. The models themselves are
black boxes and the output is very
sensitive to the input.
Verification is mandatory in the AC/DC,
not optional. Like context, verification
is an area that can become problematic
quickly. Many of the easy approaches to
verification, such as using LLMs to
check their own work, can generate a
high level of false positives and are
neither explainable nor consistent.
While they can be helpful, these
inherently imprecise approaches have to
be grounded in deterministic,
comprehensive, and transparent analyses
to maximize signal and meet enterprise
standards. They have to make it clear to
the developer who is accountable for the
work precisely what was checked, what
worked, and what did not work. There are
many valuable sources of verification
data. Deterministic code analysis such
as that produced by SonarQube is a
vital component. LLM based AI code
review is a helpful addition, but its
non-deterministic and non-comprehensive
nature means that it has to be a
complement, not a replacement. Inside of
whatever sandbox your agents work in,
the code can be tested and observability
traces generated to produce additional
information. A comprehensive
verification platform aggregates and
intermediates these signals and
ultimately will pass judgment on the end
Now those are the required pieces of the
AC/DC. We think that there are also some
emerging best practices that demonstrably
improve overall agentic performance. For
example, embedded context models are a
reflection of their training data and
techniques. And while the foundation
model companies are continuously updating
their training data, the vast majority of
that training data is based on open
source code. The quality, style, and
standards used for these open source data
sets are highly varied. And perhaps most
importantly, they are different from what
you and your company want and have used
in the past. Fine-tuning models where
the model provider allows helps to both
improve absolute quality and security
while also having the models better
reflect the context embedded in your
codebase. As agent centric development
progresses, we believe there will be an
increasing recognition of the need for
these fine-tuned enterprise models. This
is complementary to, not competitive with,
the more transient task-specific context
from the context engine I mentioned
earlier. We will see more and more
special purpose agents. Today the
baseline foundation models generate a
lot of excitement. However, addressing
specific problems in software
development likely requires smaller
models and agents that are custom-built
for purpose. A code repair agent with
custom workflows and understanding of
the verification context can better
address the solve part of the AC/DC. Code
review agents trained on pull request
information are likely to provide
developers with better information than
a generic review agent. This space is
emerging and is worth watching and
experimenting with. Now we all know
these changes cannot happen overnight.
Enterprises have existing code, existing
systems of record, existing workflows,
existing tooling. It is not only
impractical, it is ill-advised to rush
head first into the AC/DC.
There are some practical steps you can
take to get started.
First, build your verification platform.
As I mentioned, verification is
mandatory, not optional. Our best
customers who are adopting agents to
write code at scale insist and require
that 100% of the code written by AI is
reviewed by SonarQube. Second, enable
automatic tool calling. You need to make
your verification tools and your context
available in the way that the agents
will consume them: MCP servers, hooks,
programmatic tool calling. Make sure
you're engaging at the speed of the
agents. Most processes need to speed up,
and the model providers and the agent
providers are providing the capabilities
to trigger the tools that you need.
Third, employ agentic approaches to
opportunistic hunting and solving your
backlogs. Hunter agents can catch things
that other techniques miss, amplifying
your bug bounty programs. Remediation
agents, on the other hand, can work
through your backlogs, improving your
baseline posture.
With these simple steps, the speed of
progress you can make on AC/DC is truly
shocking. Now,
Sonar's roots are in the code quality
verification space.
Our focus has always been on having the
highest true positive rate and the
lowest false positive rate in the
industry while maintaining broad
coverage of languages and issues across
reliability, maintainability, and
security. Of course, we are not sitting
still. We are intensely focused on being
the foundational platform for the agent-
centric development cycle. Here's how
our current and emerging initiatives map
to the AC/DC framework. For each of our
customers, we have a deep level of
insight into your codebase, its
structure, architecture, and data flows.
Through our quality profiles, we also
capture and understand what's important
to your specific organization: whether
you're enforcing standards set by your
CIO or looking to achieve MISRA or Cyber
Resilience Act compliance. One step is
dynamic context. We have launched our
public beta of our context augmentation
service which captures your guardrails,
architectural intent and other vital
elements of context about your codebase
to feed into your agents. We also have
in private beta our embedded context
offering which we call Sonar Sweep.
Sonar Sweep provides an industry-leading
approach to embedding your existing
codebase context into a custom model, a
fine-tuned model, providing a correct-at-
the-source level of assurance.
Fine-tuning your models on a swept
version of your codebase can materially
reduce the number of security issues and
bugs that the models produce. Next:
Evolving far beyond our roots in
traditional static code analysis, we use
deterministic mathematical reasoning
methods to provide transparent,
consistent, and repeatable results that
meet corporate and regulatory needs. In
our core, we're actively employing AI to
enhance the value we can provide to you.
We use a variety of AI enhanced
techniques to improve the breadth and
quality of our reasoning algorithms.
We employ embedded AI to find and
further reduce false positives beyond
our current industry-leading levels. And
coming soon, we're using AI to help
triage and prioritize issues
specifically for you given your unique
context and codebase.
Our users have historically been the
millions of developers who have
SonarQube embedded in their workflows.
Moving forward, we will also be used by
the agents who are writing code for you.
So we're upgrading our analyzers to
optimize them for agentic analysis. This
is big and will allow SonarQube to be
used in self-verification of agents.
But we are not stopping with our core.
We believe that the best verification
platforms will leverage deterministic
systems combined thoughtfully with a
range of other verification techniques
including LLM based capabilities. For
example, we have hunting agents in early
access designed to use LLM based
reasoning techniques to
opportunistically spot check code bases
for security issues and bugs. Our early
tests have caught over 200 CVEs in open
source projects. Now, let's move on to
Solve. SonarQube has long been focused
on ensuring that our findings are
actionable and identified issues are
resolved as quickly as possible.
Past efforts focusing on adopting a
clean as you code methodology and learn
as you code content were developer-
focused initiatives to improve codebase
quality and health. These philosophies
are still incredibly valuable in an
AI-centric world and we're enhancing them
specifically for the AC/DC. AI code fix is
available as a default capability inside
of SonarQube enterprise plans, allowing
you to bring your favorite LLMs to
suggest fixes to issues identified by
And SonarQube remediation agent now in
public beta builds on our benchmark-
leading Sonar Foundation agent to
automatically remediate a large number
of issues identified during the
All of this is seamlessly enabled for
tool use within Claude Code, Google
anti-gravity, Cursor, and other AI
coding tools using SonarQube MCP server
as well as a now available SonarQube
Put together, Sonar provides the

and reliably power any of your chosen
code generation tools and agents. So
that's a view of the future and our road
map. We have high ambitions because we
know that you have high ambitions. We're
moving quickly because we know that you
Throughout the Sonar Summit, our teams
will go into a lot more detail. There's
over 50 sessions for you to enjoy.
Throughout those sessions, we'll all

want to close the way I started with a
thank you. It's a real privilege and a

of you. So, thank you. and I hope you'll

I want to say at the outset on behalf of all Sonar sourcers, thank you. Sonar has been a developer oriented company since our beginning as an open source project. It is comprehensive looking at your entire codebase and understanding not just the literal code but the architecture and the data flows. Really whatever helps you verify and trust your code. This could be because of best practice, personal preference, or compliance. Whether you're looking to certify for MISRA, NIST, secure software development framework, SOC 2, or a range of other regulations, we help you enforce these profiles with quality gates, ensuring that the code that you or your AI checks in meets the standards you've set. We do this with the lowest false positive rates in the industry, less than 3%. And we do it with advice, guidance, and AI-powered suggestions about how you can address any issues that are found.
